frames.description: ground truth — what is really happening at Brae Hollow across both ages
Sera, the Founding-Age well-keeper at the Cistern Steps; knows the young hollow and her own corner only
Halden, the Founding-Age dyer-and-boundary-mason in the Loom Quarter; knows the craft-row dispute, never the drought to come
the traveller with the wend-pipe; the only one who crosses between the two ages and sees both causes
Ode, the Withering-Age field-warden at the Wend Fields; inherits a hollow she did not build and cannot see the cause of
Marn, the Withering-Age well-tender at the Cistern Steps; keeps the last good water without knowing why it still runs
Pell, the Withering-Age weaver at the last loom in the quiet Loom Quarter; knows nothing of the old dispute
Tamsin, the Withering-Age far-field settler who reaches the far field across the bridge-tree (planted future only)

branches.description: PLANTED future — the bearer planted the gorge-sapling; a living bridge-tree spans the gorge and the far field is reachable
BARREN future — the bearer did not plant; nothing grew, the gorge stays impassable and the far field is lost

entities.description: the Cistern Steps — the upper spring and its stone catch-basin (the water); same ground in both ages
the Loom Quarter — the craft-row of dyers, weavers, a boundary-mason (the work); same ground in both ages
the Wend Fields — the lower farmland and the river crossing (the living); same ground in both ages
the cistern's lower vent — the load-bearing prop of the Cistern Steps; the same stone touched in both ages
Halden's boundary-stone — the load-bearing prop of the Loom Quarter chain; the same stone in both ages, half-buried by the late age
the slow gorge-sapling — the load-bearing prop of the Wend Fields fork; planted in the Founding Age it becomes the Withering-Age bridge-tree
the carved bone wend-pipe — the time-toggle the bearer carries to move between ages on one spot
the cistern itself as a persisting state-bearer: its condition (rotting / sealed-clean) carries across the ages
the river ford as a persisting state-bearer: its condition (unmarked / marked-firm) carries across the ages
Sera, the Founding well-keeper (as a referenced figure)
Halden, the Founding dyer-mason (as a referenced figure)
the bearer, the traveller (as a referenced figure)
Ode, the Withering field-warden (as a referenced figure)
Marn, the Withering well-tender (as a referenced figure)
Pell, the Withering weaver (as a referenced figure)
Tamsin, the Withering far-field settler (as a referenced figure)

predicates.description: the persisting physical condition of a state-object (cistern: rotting/sealed-clean; ford: unmarked/marked-firm); the exclusive rule keys off this
which person holds a carried prop (the bearer holds the wend-pipe, then the gorge-sapling)

facts.claim: In the Founding Age Brae Hollow is young and thriving: the spring runs, the craft-row clatters, the river crossing is busy
Sera keeps the upper spring and its stone catch-basin at the Cistern Steps in the young hollow
In the Founding Age a creeping rot works into the rock at the cistern's lower vent
Sera finds the creeping rot at the cistern's lower vent and knows it will foul the water if left
In the Founding Age Sera packs and seals the cistern's lower vent against the creeping rot
Sera packs the lower vent tight against the rot, trusting the cistern to hold for whoever keeps it after her
In the Founding Age Sera cuts her keeper's mark in the stone beside the sealed vent so a later hand will know it was tended
Sera cuts her keeper's mark beside the vent, a sign for some keeper she will never meet
With the vent sealed the cistern runs sweet again and Sera's work at the Steps is done
In the Founding Age the Loom Quarter craft-row is busy with dyers, weavers, and Halden the boundary-mason
A craft-row dispute breaks out over which crossing the dye-carts may use across the river
In the Founding Age the only ford that will stay firm when the channel shifts is unmarked and unknown to all but a careful reader of the river
Halden reads the river and judges which ford will hold firm when the channel shifts in years to come
Halden cuts a boundary-stone to mark the firm ford, settling the dye-cart dispute in stone
In the Founding Age Halden sets the boundary-stone at the head of the firm ford, marking the crossing that will stay firm when the river shifts
Halden sets the boundary-stone for a craft-row dispute, never imagining a drought, only the carts
In the Founding Age the Wend Fields are green farmland and the river crossing is busy
The bearer arrives in the young hollow carrying the carved bone wend-pipe
The bearer carries the wend-pipe, the carved bone whistle that moves them between the ages
At the Cistern Steps the bearer watches Sera pack and seal the lower vent against the rot
At the Loom Quarter the bearer watches Halden set the boundary-stone at the head of the firm ford
The bearer learns the wend-pipe, played on a spot, moves them between the two ages standing on the same ground
The bearer walks down to the Wend Fields and the river crossing in the young hollow
At the crossing a dry gorge cuts the way to the far field across the channel
A field-elder gives the bearer a slow gorge-sapling and tells what it could become over generations
The bearer plays the wend-pipe and stands a moment in the withered hollow to come, the spring near the last water
The bearer crosses back and understands the early age is the late age's cause: what is done now lands generations on
The bearer returns to the Wend crossing with the gorge-sapling still in hand
The bearer weighs whether the far field across the gorge is worth the planting
The bearer kneels at the lip of the dry gorge at dusk, the sapling ready
At the Wend crossing in the Founding Age the bearer plants the slow gorge-sapling in the gorge wall
The bearer plants the gorge-sapling and trusts the slow tree to span the gorge for an age it will never see
At the Wend crossing in the Founding Age the bearer does not plant the gorge-sapling and walks on
The bearer keeps the sapling unplanted, leaving the gorge to whatever the late age makes of bare rock
Three generations on, the Withering Age lies on Brae Hollow under a long drought, the spring near the last water in the valley
Marn the well-tender keeps the cistern at the Cistern Steps, the last good water in the valley
Marn does not know why the cistern still runs clean when every other spring in the valley has failed
The bearer crosses to the Withering Steps and recognises the same vent Sera packed three generations gone
Three generations on the cistern still runs clean — the last good water in the valley — because Sera sealed the vent
The bearer finds Sera's keeper's mark still cut in the stone beside the clean-running vent
The Loom Quarter has gone quiet; Pell the weaver works the last loom on the dead craft-row
Pell knows nothing of the old craft-row dispute or who cut any boundary-stone
Ode the field-warden keeps the crossing at the Wend Fields for the Withering folk
Ode finds a boundary-stone half-buried at the shifted channel and reads it as the mark of the firm ford
The Withering folk cross to the last green field on the firm ford the boundary-stone marks, because a dyer marked it in an age none of them remember
The gorge-sapling the bearer planted has grown to a living bridge-tree spanning the dry gorge
Tamsin the far-field settler reaches and works the far field across the living bridge-tree
The bearer crosses to the Founding Age and witnesses why the cistern runs and the ford holds: Sera's seal and Halden's stone
Brae Hollow withers but the far field carries it: the water holds, the ford holds, and the bridge-tree holds
Three generations on, the Withering Age lies on Brae Hollow under a long drought, the spring near the last water in the valley
Marn the well-tender keeps the cistern at the Cistern Steps, the last good water in the valley
Marn does not know why the cistern still runs clean when every other spring in the valley has failed
The bearer crosses to the Withering Steps and recognises the same vent Sera packed three generations gone
Three generations on the cistern still runs clean — the last good water in the valley — because Sera sealed the vent
The bearer finds Sera's keeper's mark still cut in the stone beside the clean-running vent
The Loom Quarter has gone quiet; Pell the weaver works the last loom on the dead craft-row
Pell knows nothing of the old craft-row dispute or who cut any boundary-stone
Ode the field-warden keeps the crossing at the Wend Fields for the Withering folk
Ode finds a boundary-stone half-buried at the shifted channel and reads it as the mark of the firm ford
The Withering folk cross to the near green field on the firm ford the boundary-stone marks, because a dyer marked it in an age none of them remember
At the gorge nothing grew; the crossing-place to the far field is bare rock
The dry gorge is impassable and the far field across it is lost to the hollow
Ode marks the far field as unreachable: no bridge spans the gorge and the hollow must live on the near field alone
The bearer crosses to the Founding Age and witnesses why the cistern runs and the ford holds: Sera's seal and Halden's stone
Brae Hollow withers on the near field alone; the water and ford hold but the far field stays lost
